Accountability
To micromanage
Autonomy

This quarter, I'm going to have Oskar lead the negotiations.
I got him to take ownership of the whole account.
I had the rate sheets reviewed before the meeting.
You wanted to talk about the supplier negotiations this quarter?
I did. This time, I'm not going to lead them. I'm going to have you run the whole process.
Me? I've sat in on them, but I've never led one myself. That's a steep learning curve.
That's exactly why. You have the potential; you just need the ownership. I'll mentor you, but I'll stay hands-off.
What if I get a rate wrong? These deals are big.
Then we fix it together. I'd rather get you to make one small mistake now than micromanage you forever. Autonomy is how you grow.
Okay, I'll take it on. Could you have the last rate sheets sent to me so I can prepare?
Already done -- I had them forwarded this morning. Step up, and I'll be right behind you.
I'm going to have you run it.
I got him to take ownership.
I had the report reviewed.
I'll have the sheets forwarded.
I'm going to have you run the whole process.
I want to get you to take ownership of this.
I'll mentor you, but I'll stay hands-off.
I had the rate sheets forwarded to you.
Step up on this one, and I'll be right behind you.

When I was first promoted, I did what most new managers do. I kept doing the work myself, only faster, because I was good at it. And for a while it looked like it was working. But my team wasn't growing, and I was exhausted. The change came when I stopped doing and started developing. Now, instead of leading every negotiation, I have my best people run them. I get them to make the real decisions, not just carry my bag. I give them ownership, and yes, I stay hands-off, even when it's uncomfortable. Of course they make mistakes. Last month I had a junior lead a supplier call, and it didn't go perfectly. So we debriefed, and this week she's leading it again. People think that's a failure of delegation. It isn't. That is delegation working. The strongest teams are built by leaders who are brave enough to get the work done through others, and patient enough to let them learn.

Welcome to this short segment on the causative, the grammar of getting things done through other people. There are two situations to keep apart. First, when a person does something for you. Use have plus the person plus the base verb, with no to. I had her present the results. Or use get plus the person plus to plus the verb. I got him to take ownership. Same idea, but get needs the little word to, and have does not. Second, when a thing is done for you, and it doesn't matter who does it. Use have or get plus the object plus the past participle. I had the report reviewed. I got the slides redesigned. The trick is to notice whether you are talking about a person doing something or a thing being done, and then choose the right form. Master this, and you can talk about delegation like a leader.
